title: Smoothing Out OPC Extensions: From Hardcoded meta.nodes to Capability Contracts
summary: The first version of OPC's extension system worked, but activation was hardcoded as `meta.nodes = ["code-review"]`‚Äîadding a new flow meant changing extension code, creating tight coupling. After rewriting it with capability contracts (provides √ó requires), extensions no longer know OPC node names. Also fixed 4 gotchas that would frustrate users.
body:
## Background

OPC (One-Person Company) is an agent framework I built‚Äîeach flow is a chain of nodes, with subagents running between nodes, reviewing each other, and finally a judge scoring the results. I wanted to add an extension mechanism: let specialist tools like `design-lint` and `visual-eval` hook into specific nodes.

The first version shipped in 48 hours and worked. Then the metaphor judge gave "non-hardcoded activation" a 2/5. The architecture agent diagnosed further:

> `meta.nodes = ["code-review", "acceptance"]` is hardcoded. Extensions know OPC node names. Adding a new flow or changing a node name means coming back to modify extension code. Tight coupling.

My first instinct was to argue‚Äîthe `meta.nodes` array only has 3 elements, what's the big deal with changing it? But I knew the judge was right: **This isn't about the size of technical debt, it's about architectural direction.** Right now 3 nodes and 3 extensions barely work; with 10 flows and 5 extensions you get an N√óM coupling matrix.

## Redesign: Capability Contract

Inspiration came from Linux `/proc/capabilities`, SELinux capabilities, and TypeScript's structural typing. The core principle is simple:

> Neither side knows the other directly‚Äîthey only share a common vocabulary.

Applied to OPC:

- **Node side**: Flow template declares `nodeCapabilities["code-review"] = ["code-quality-check", "visual-consistency-check"]`
- **Extension side**: hook.mjs declares `meta.provides = ["visual-consistency-check"]`
- **Harness side**: Triggers when `ext.meta.provides ‚à© node.nodeCapabilities ‚âÝ ‚àÖ`

The core matching logic is three lines of JS:

```js
const requires = new Set(ctx.nodeCapabilities ?? []);
const provides = ext.meta.provides ?? [];
const fires = provides.some(p => requires.has(p));
```

Extensions no longer know any OPC node names. I can add a new node called `post-launch-sim`‚Äîas long as it declares `["user-simulation"]`, the `visual-eval` extension automatically fires on it. No extension code changes required.

## Governance Layer: CAPABILITIES.md

Capability names are free-form strings. Runtime doesn't validate‚Äîtypo one character (`vissual-consistency-check`) and it silently won't fire. That's the price of KISS.

The solution isn't adding validation, it's adding **governance docs**. I wrote `~/.opc/extensions/CAPABILITIES.md` with a canonical capability table: `code-quality-check`, `visual-consistency-check`, `user-simulation`, `security-check`, `a11y-check`. PRs that add new capabilities must first add a line to this table. Governance through convention, not code‚Äîjust like HTTP status codes.

## Smoothing: Four User-Breaking Gotchas

While refactoring, I filled several holes the old version had been hiding. Each one looks like a small bug individually, but together they create the difference between "works when you click" and "explodes when you click."

### Gotcha 1: `.git` Directory Loaded as Extension

Extensions live in `~/.opc/extensions/`, each subdirectory is an extension. My initial scan logic was:

```js
const dirs = readdirSync(base);  // List all subdirectories
for (const d of dirs) { await loadExt(join(base, d)); }
```

Then I `git clone` this repo, and `.git/` ended up in the dirs array. `loadExt` reports "can't find hook.mjs", throws, harness FATAL.

Fix: Skip dotfiles and require `hook.mjs` to exist‚Äîtwo lines that should've been there from day one.

### Gotcha 2: Hook Has No timeout

`verdictAppend(ctx)` calls `execFileSync(ENGINE, ..., { timeout: 60_000 })`‚Äîthe engine process has timeout, but **the hook function itself** (the `await` on child_process) has no timeout. If a hook author writes an infinite `await new Promise(() => {})`, the harness hangs forever.

Fix: Add a `withTimeout(promise, ms)` wrapper using `Promise.race`, timeout warns + continues. Default 60s, overridable with env var `OPC_HOOK_TIMEOUT_MS`.

### Gotcha 3: Hook Returns Wrong Type and Crashes

`promptAppend` expects to return `string`. Hook author accidentally `return 42`, or `return undefined`, or `return Promise`‚Äîharness directly calls `result.includes(...)` and crashes.

Fix: Type guards. Return non-string gets warned + treated as `""`. Same for `verdictAppend`‚Äîmust be array, elements must have `severity/category/message` fields. Legacy `{ emoji, text, file }` format gets auto-normalized with a one-time warning for authors to migrate.

### Gotcha 4: Dual `normalizeHook` Implementation

Hook files can be written in three formats (default export + hooks object, named export + camelCase, named export + dot-notation key). All three must be supported (historical reasons). I had two normalize codebases: one in `extensions.mjs` for runtime, one in `extension-test` CLI for dev. The two got out of sync‚Äîhooks that passed CLI testing would explode at runtime.

Fix: Extract canonical `normalizeHook(raw, mod)`, `export` it, both CLI and runtime import this one version. Deleted 70 lines of duplicate code.

## Verification: 40 Unit Tests + 2 Smoke Tests

After all changes, ran tests: `node --test bin/lib/extensions.test.mjs` ‚Üí 40 tests passed, 0 failed.

Coverage:
- `loadExtensions` various dir pollution / required vs optional / extensionOrder
- Capability matching: match / no match / empty provides / empty requires / multiple
- Timeout: slow hooks pass, bomb hooks get killed
- Legacy format compatibility
- Registry cache roundtrip
- Benchmark bypass (`OPC_DISABLE_EXTENSIONS=1` / whitelist / priority)

Two smoke tests (real flows):
1. `code-review` node (requires `["visual-consistency-check"]`) ‚Üí `design-lint` extension's prompt appears in subagent context
2. `build` node (no `nodeCapabilities` declared) ‚Üí no extensions fire, clean stdout

## Reflection: Things That Should've Been Right in V1

- **Activation is a contract, not a name list**. Any design where "A knows B's name" is a smell.
- **Anywhere that can throw must warn + continue**. Extensions aren't core functionality‚Äîif they explode, they can't drag the whole run down.
- **Type guards belong on the consumer side**. Hook authors making mistakes is normal‚Äîruntime catching them is my responsibility.
- **Governance layer is independent of code layer**. Free-form capability names are a feature‚Äî`CAPABILITIES.md` is governance, not validation code.

The difference between an agent framework that "works" and one that's "usable" isn't features‚Äîit's **thoughtfulness toward users**. Every time a user gets frustrated, they use you one less time. These frameworks don't die from bugs, they die from "I can't be bothered anymore."
